14 Growth + Marketing AMA with Amara Omoregie  04 12 22 - YouTube.mp4
And I've been in marketing shortly. joined marketing shortly after that. I worked for several agencies. We've worked with Fortune 10 companies, all the way to Fortune 500, Fortune 1000. But my favorite companies to work for are small businesses, which is why I started my agency. I love helping bring marketing operations and sales operations to small businesses to help scale their businesses. So. On these marketing calls, bring any and every question that you have. So it's not just funnel hacking. It's not just, what do I do about my marketing? If you're having trouble hiring people for marketing, if you're having trouble with actual operations, with trying to figure out technology solutions and things like that, we're here to talk about. The things that business owners and marketing teams struggle with.
You know, day to day, week to week. As they're trying to, you know, position themselves for scale. Feel free to drop, like Carly said, feel free to drop a question in the chat. Now, I know a lot of folks are kind of like, 'I just want to listen in and kind of hear.' Well, you know, I mean, yeah, you might learn a lot from that, but really it's, you know, bring your questions because that's super valuable. This is an opportunity to get real, unbiased advice. Like I, you know, you guys aren't my necessarily my clients. So I'm not trying to sell you anything. This is just like real, pure, honest, what I would do if I were in your shoes— type information, um, that I'm giving you guys and actionable also.
So I'm not going to tell you to go spend a million dollars to make 50 bucks type stuff. I'm going to. Try to tell you guys things that you guys can do tomorrow. In your businesses to move the needle or get you over the hurdle that you guys are overcoming. So my understanding is that you guys just started your cohort. And I'm just wasting time for the next couple minutes while new people jump in and while you guys ask your questions. So while you guys are just getting started on your cohort, I don't believe you guys have started mapping your growth engines yet. That's exciting. Please, for next week, bring those questions for sure. Cause those are always fun to go through growth engines, figure out where things might be a little too complicated.
Um, and so on and so forth. I look at growth engines before growth engines were a thing. I look at them every single day. Troubleshoot. Unclog them and make them work better all the time. So this is your chance to get some really great info. At least I think it's great. We'll see. I'll let y'all be the judge of that. It is great info and always valuable. Amara, thank you so much. Okay, sweet. Let's get the ball rolling. It looks like a few questions have come in. So let's start up with Valerie. She says, 'What question will you ask to hire sales people that set sales calls?' Ooh, good question. So it depends. So it's kind of, so the way I'm going to answer that question is actually throw it back on you.
So. There's a lot of issues with teams that hire salespeople that aren't prepared. So. It kind of depends on what kind of salesperson it is. Is it an outbound salesperson or an inbound salesperson? I'll need to find that so that I don't use jargon. So an inbound salesperson is you have leads coming in and then you have salespeople answering those leads. And you want those salespeople to convert those leads, right? Outbound salesperson is you have a customer, a buyer persona, and a customer profile. Their job is to prospect, which means go out, build a list of people that they're going to, that they want to target, meet with those people, close those, and have a certain number of meetings. And then obviously close a certain amount of business.
A lot of times outbound salespeople already have a Rolodex of people that they can work from. They don't just start from here. Nothing unless they're absolutely crazy, which there are some. That loved the challenge of that but most have some sort of light up— they're like, 'I've worked in this industry for years, I know where these people live, where what they eat, where the kids you know play soccer, whatever.' And so they have this massive pool of people. Typically, the successful ones have this massive pull of this type of persona in their back pocket that they know how to talk to and know how to market to. Um, so asking that question again, is it an outbound salesperson or is it an inbound salesperson? Looks like you can come off mute, Valerie.
So basically, it's inbound. Mm-hmm. But the role of that person, I was told to put somewhere, someone between when the lead arrived and myself. To qualify the lead and also get the lead excited and then I do just the closing of the sales. I do the sales in a two-step instead of doing the sales in a one-step. Okay. So, I mean, so if you're looking to hire a salesperson, that's simply there to qualify. Um... I mean, as long as they have good people skills, there's really not a whole, you don't even really need a salesperson for that. You just need someone that likes to talk to people, because you're not actually closing and selling unless you want them to close and sell. Um, Realistically, I know you have been knowing what we talked about last week, Valerie.
If you've been doing all the closing and all the selling, if you write down your process or at least have recordings of those calls that people can listen to and write down your process and what you're doing in those key steps of the process, like 'Okay, lead comes in So then the lead comes in, and then what? Do we send them an email? Do we confirm the meeting? Whatever. Okay, great. They confirm the lead. We have the meeting. What are the five things we go over in that meeting? What are the things that we're looking for? Here's the questions that we ask. On that call and then we get a yes or no. If that's your process, I'm way oversimplifying it, then that's literally all that person needs to do.
So you have your KPIs that you benchmark. So for every lead that comes in, you know that you can close. I don't know, 20%. I made that up. So. They need to be able to meet that or exceed that. And you're doing 10 different jobs, right? So in reality, they shouldn't do worse than that. Given if they have your exact script and the exact steps that you take. Because here's the thing about salespeople. If they follow your exact method and if you have a benchmark in place, then— and you're already selling— then they should succeed period. End if you don't have a system in place and you're trying to make them invent sales for you, then that's a totally different story. You're not doing that.
In my opinion, if they can follow a process and. All you would need to do is then spot check their calls or make sure the calls are recorded. Spot check their calls, do coaching with them and make sure that they're saying the right things on those calls. Um, so that they can either qualify them for you, or heck— you might even be doing way too many extra steps. They might even be able to just— they might even just be able to close them. Because really, you're saying they're saying the same thing. I'm listening for those same things. Okay. And what question will you ask them at that point to hire them? Um, I mean, the qualities that you're looking for in a salesperson is someone who can, you know.
Have they used a CRM before? Do you have a CRM set up for this? Yeah. Do they know how to like? Um. How do they handle follow-up? Are they familiar with follow-up? Like, get familiar with their follow-up process and how they follow up with leads. If you have a follow-up process you want them to follow. You know, what does a, you know, get an idea what a productive day looks like for them as a salesperson. How they set up their day. That's super important for a salesperson because. A salesperson that's all over the place will say something like... Oh, I just write down people I want to talk to and, you know, I just keep a list and, you know. Um. A productive salesperson will be pretty organized.
So like, you know, I'll have my new leads I need to talk to. I'll um, you know, have the follow-up list that I want to talk to. I'll follow up with those. I'll do some prospecting. I'll do my reporting, et cetera, et cetera. They'll be pretty organized about it. Um, you know, I would maybe ask them. Uh, how do they note, how do they make notes about the conversations they're having with prospects? Do they have a method that they use? The more thorough they are, the better. So you're not having to hunt them down and say, 'What happened with this lead?' 'What happened to that lead?' Just kind of knowing their process and kind of how they work their leads and just making sure they're organized and they understand the assignment.
As far as like what you need in order to properly manage them so that they can you can make sure that they're effective. and so on and so forth. But I mean, really, even if they had no experience. If they're disqualifying and they're not being forced to sell, an admin could that knows how to talk to people and can clearly communicate about what it is you guys offer and ask the questions should be able to do it. Awesome. Thank you. You're welcome. Was that helpful? Yes. Okay, cool. Awesome. Thanks, Valerie. All right. Next is from Jack. He says, I own buybackboss. com. We are a device trading company and have only had success with bottom of the funnel marketing. But this style of marketing leads to a lot of cost-sensitive customers.
How do you think we should market to them at the top of the funnel? Oh my gosh, we did a campaign for a company like this. What company was it? It was here in LA, Orange County. Thank you. Oh my God, I cannot think of the name of the company. That was a tough one. Um. This is like 13 years ago. Like a really long time ago. Um... It was a gazelle. No, it was. Oh my God, I can't think of the name. I can see the logo on my head. But I can't— they were one of my clients that I took on right before I left the agency that I was with. It wasn't at my agency. It was my old agency— I used to work at.
That was actually 15 years ago. I'm dating myself. Yeah, that one was tough. We used to run ads for them. And I think. Our ads had to be less than eight. Their cost per lead had to be under $8 at Top of Funnel. And this was, again, 15 years ago. And phones were a lot less expensive back then. And they didn't get as much resale as they do now. So I imagine things have changed quite a bit. Um, especially with you know how logistics go and you know sending the box and getting it back and all that good stuff. Which is assuming what you guys do, correct? Yeah, that's the same business model, but the marketing budget on a per device or per order basis is still very tight. Okay.
So that hasn't changed. At cost of Ghana. Yeah. Um, I mean... yeah, no it's that is a really tough tough tough market. Um... So, you know, I always go whenever I run into this type of issue, it really. You have to go back to the details. So one of the great things about Scalable. Um, you're probably going to end up doing is when you build your growth engines. Have you built buyer personas or anything like that for your, for your company? Yeah, we've attempted to, but whenever we look into the data of who buys from us, it's like, you know. From a holistic basis, it's anyone who's got a device to trade in. And so it's really spread as far as any of the demographics that are selling to us or the situation the person's in.
In general, it's like someone upgrading their phone, obviously. That's a huge portion of who's selling to us, especially when there's a time for Apple upgrading season or things like that. But we have had trouble nailing down a specific buyer persona for that reason. Right. So in your case, I would go back to exactly what you just said. Don't segment your persona based on demographics. I get segment your persona based on The moment of truth. I talk about this a lot on the calls. Google championed a study called the zero moment of truth. What's the moment that someone triggers? What's the trigger that causes someone to want to make a change or do something different? Or do the thing that would make them want to look for your company.
So, if it's upgrading, if it's my phone that no longer works. What do I do? What are those key things and make personas around that. And then what you'll want to do is figure out what their what their journey is, right? Like, I do customer experience research. That clearly maps out, okay, my phone stopped working. I'm going to research what. My options are for troubleshooting, maybe. So are you showing up? When they're evaluating their options, when their phone stops working. Right. Um, they love their phone, but they don't want to, they don't want to get rid of it. Like, what are their options? So. One of their options is to go get it repaired. Another option is to trade it in and get something new.
So, at least be in that space, top of funnel space when they're making that. So that's where you need to go: top of funnel, instead of wide neck. Um, trying to get everybody, so when you're a little bit more specific, that way and you can pull out those key triggers. And really just make sure that you are where your customers are looking for alternatives or just evaluating their options. A lot of companies miss out— just not even being in that evaluation stage— when they're looking at options. So when you're doing your content strategy and things like that, just make content around. Those key triggers and that could help you really be more competitive, because everybody's just kind of going for the easy, low-hanging fruit. Well, that's like the most competitive part.
So, for in response to that, like with the from a content perspective, so a lot of our content was previously like targeting, like, 'sell iPhone,' and then we did a big push, like, on our blog, to target, like, that moment of truth, like, what situation would you be in when you're looking to actually trade in something, like, you said, when it broke, you're having an issue with your device, put out like hundreds of like troubleshooting articles. And we, in like a six-month timeframe, we got maybe like 150,000 visits extra to our website for those articles. So I would consider it a decent six. As far as like getting the traffic. But from a conversion perspective, like we are getting that traffic, but we're not converting a lot of those customers because they are top of the funnel.
Do you have any recommendations for how to bring that customer into a kind of a commoditized market so we can't really use, like, an e-book or anything like that? Like people aren't looking for information other than hitting them with a coupon and those sorts of things within that blog post? Because that's what we currently do. We kind of say, 'Hey, here's the blog post. By the way, we buy stuff if you want it. Then we'll hit him with a coupon later down the funnel.' Are you guys doing any retargeting with, like, display? Yeah, we do a bit of retargeting with display when we've tried it on Facebook and things like the dollar cost was just not doing the trick for us. And I don't know if that was a messaging issue.
or what it was, but we've tried a million different ways of retargeting and we have a lot of trouble getting the conversions from the top of the funnel people we mainly use it for— someone that liked typing in 'cell iphone' looking to sell the device and then we retarget them to say, 'Hey, we're one of the 10 companies you like. Price you check. Price on Okay.' Um, and this is Google at Google Display. Uh, yes. Okay. Cause it's pretty inexpensive. Have you tried doing native ads? Like Outbreak, Taboola, or anything like that? Not sure on that. I can check on that. I'll write that down. Have you tried Pinterest? I kid you not. I kid you not. So I had this client. That had like the stupid high budget to do anything I wanted.
And I'm like, let me try Pinterest. And they had this boring loan brand. I mean, the most boring loan brand you could think of. That had really predatory loans that anyone really want to work with them that's beside the point. Um, It was a friend's company, so whatever. But anyway. I was like, let me try Pinterest. So I started doing some organic posts on Pinterest and then I ran some ads. I don't know why it converted the best, but it did. There's some seriously high intent on Pinterest and it's not very crowded. So, um... I don't know if you'd have like a little bit of extra budget laying around— you want to test it. Tested test Pinterest throws some weird and really great um stuff your way, believe it or not.
They are pretty targeted and they're super high intent on that platform. Um. And it's not that expensive either. So I would maybe even try that. All right, sweet. Thank you very much. As far as converting top of funnel, top of funnel is just really difficult, but however. For the people that you got all that traffic from, you should be able to convert. So more of that type of traffic, I think. What is it that your competitors offer that you don't? Or is there something that you can do to get an edge over them to make them choose you? Over someone else or is it like a timing thing? Is it a trust factor? Have you guys, like, surveyed or looked into it at all? It's not necessarily that people are offering more than we are.
We're, we're one of the original people in the buyback space actually. And we're one of the, we're in one of the midsize closer to the largest company in this space. It's more of an issue of everything's commoditized and it's based on pricing. Like someone's like, well, this person will give me $201. This person will give me $200. There's not a lot of value adds, even when you try to add like service offerings or things like that onto the deal. So that's kind of the issue we have: you're getting them bottom of the funnel or someone's saying 'sell.' iPhone, they compare 10 buyback websites, and they just take them with the highest price and has decent reviews. But it's like me and my top four competitors have the same 4.
8 out of five review on Trustpilot or whatever, and the same pricing and all these things. So it just becomes a price war at that point, and whoever's operations are more in tune. Okay. Huh. I'm wondering if there's anything in addition that you can offer them, or is there any questions people ask? Are they afraid of something like insurance? Or anything like that. I don't know, that would be something that I would maybe. uh, Think about like. I think whoever knows their customer the best is going to win, and I think it's just going to get to the minutia. Like. Sold a phone like this. Like is this your first time? Don't worry. Here's the guarantees that we have in place to make sure that we don't just run off with your phone or, let's say, your phone gets lost in the mail. Here's the guarantees that we have. That would be my worry. I would never use this type of service for that reason. I would just walk into my T-Mobile and... hand in my phone and hope for the best, even if I'm not getting as much back as I would want to. Give you a list of opportunities for improvement. And have some ideas for what they think could improve it. And even if they don't act on it right this second. You they have a Thank you. plan for what they want to do next, next, next. As things evolve. So if they're waiting on you for that info, then you might not replace yourself. You just maybe have hired someone that's comfortable in the ad account. Um. And that's okay. But maybe you're going to have to build that person up to be able to have some ownership of it and come and say, 'Okay, here's our baseline now.
Here's what we want to do improve to put a plan together. To see if we can test some things. And if they can't, then again, you're probably gonna have to be a lot more involved than you'd like to be. All right, so I think the level that I have been doing media buying, I could probably run an agency myself because I'm looking after like five or six ad accounts. But these are all my own businesses.
Um, but yeah, I just wanted to— he's not that experienced; he's still pretty young and fresher— but you know, given that I'm in Asia, I can't hire a media buyer in like America, given the currency difference, you know? So I had to hire someone locally in Bangladesh. So he's a fresher, but he's willing to learn and he knows how to do Facebook ads because he's been doing it for about a year. So I can pretty much teach him. So I guess my question will be more towards. How could I? You know, sort of mentor him and grow him. As a media buyer. Replacing me and what KPIs I could set so that I guess. I guess I can get the most out of. You know, having him as a media buyer.
Yeah, I would map out your growth engine very clearly. And that way you can map out like how your traffic flows and if you have any retargeting. Uh, audiences and things like that very clearly explain those things to him. Make sure that return on ad spend, super important for e-commerce, right? I don't know what your current ROAS is. But he should definitely. Keep it there. Yeah, we're getting around 10, around like 10, 8 to 10 ROAS. For our market. Yeah, so it's pretty good. Okay. As of right now. Yeah, so do you think I should just... You know, go with the main metrics like ROAS. CPA, like this sort of thing. Or should do you think there should be some KPIs on like um on other stuff like testing.
Yeah and um I don't know like running new products. Yes. Or you know You know, I'd always had the overall. Overarching and I know like obviously CPA is going to be a target and that's a very common thing that I would obviously tell them right like CPA and ROAS and things like that. But yes, how about the overarching? Yeah. And it's a responsibility, like overarching what? What could be some KPIs or? Is he doing any on-page optimization at all? Like, is he doing like updating product descriptions or anything like that or making recommendations for those types of things? Or is he strictly, well, he just started literally two days ago, so I'm still going over the account with him. He hasn't even started like running ads yet.
So I'm just kind of wondering: Do you want him to do those things? Like, make recommendations on... like Thank you. product description updates or whatever the case may be, creative. Like making sure, yeah, yeah he's willing to do that, yeah he is willing to do that, and he's gonna be— I mean, even though he's not full-time part of our company, but he's going to be like treated like a full-time person and working directly with my creative and my content. Okay. So frequency is another one, making sure that you like whatever frequency you're comfortable with. That's a good one. Like you don't want it to go over four or five. Um, times someone's seeing your ads, you don't you want to make sure your return on ad spend stays at 10—um, doesn't go any lower than that if you guys are releasing new products every month, having a like a process for that.
Like okay, we have images, we have this and that, we launch a page so this is when you get it. And this is what you need to do to launch new products. This is how much budget I want to put behind them. And things like that. I think that would probably be a good start for them so that they kind of know what their day-to-day looks like. Um, they need to look at ad fatigue, see if any other ads are fatiguing. Um, look at like testing different types of content. With those ads. So using dynamic ads and things like that, testing new interest groups and audiences. Potentially looking at new segments and things like that. Um, Yeah, I would just make sure that your budget is earmarked for different types of things.
And he's very clear on that. And he can measure those things, and you have a very clear growth scorecard on how you want all of those, how you want all of that from your whole company to roll up into overall growth so that he has a plan. On what you're trying to do. So I think the KPIs also have a lot to do with like your overall growth strategy from the company. So it's hard to say, like, what specific additional KPIs. Those should be. But, you know, frequency, return on ad spend, cost per acquisition. Um, those types of things for sure. Got it. Okay. Thank you. Conversion rate. Conversion rate's good too. So, like, looking to see what works better than others and so on and so forth. Conversion rate's a good one.
Got it. Thank you. Okay, hopefully that was helpful. Um... Yeah, it looks like. Callie's question is up next. Good to see you, Callie. She says, 'My question is how to troubleshoot marketing in general. Our funnels and launches have been consistently underperforming over the last few years and continuing to slip. I'm not sure if the problem is more competition in our market. So many big world things going on that are affecting our customers mentally and financially.' Or if it's a people problem and I just don't have a marketing director who is skilled enough to take us forward. Ooh, juicy one. It's a deep one. All right. So who else has that problem? Anyone has hands? I want to see who else has this problem. Anyone, anyone? No, maybe not yet.
Okay. All right. Well, you're not alone. I hear this a lot. So. Woo! Um, I, I'm not the one to throw a marketing director under the bus, but I wouldn't be honest if I were saying that this isn't a common issue. Um. We used to joke that we rescue CMOs and things like that all the time and, you know, directors of marketing. Um... It's a tough position because there's so many things going on in marketing. There's new traffic channels, there's customer behavior, there's things going on in the business. And a lot of times when you have a marketing director who is not tech, like doesn't understand tactics, doesn't understand execution, doesn't understand how all the puzzles fit together. They're just going to keep trying to put bubble gum on a leaky hole and it doesn't always work.
And they often don't know that sometimes you need to just trash the whole strategy and start over again. And that's okay too. Sometimes that happens. Sometimes, whatever worked three years ago, I mean, think about it. There was a pandemic three years ago. Business has completely changed in a lot of industries three years ago. So, if your funnels haven't been performing in the last three years, and the conversation, 'Should we just try something completely new or different hasn't come up, then you might be right. You might not have— a forward-thinking enough marketing director. And so you might need to bring someone in that can look at what you have and say, 'Callie, let's. Let's start, let's try it. Let's keep doing what you're doing just to keep maintain.
But let's try some new things and see if we can't get better results there. Um... And it's, you know, unfortunately, marketing isn't magic. It's—it's— it's math. It's science. Right. And so. By understanding what our customers want and where they're at and so on and so forth. It shouldn't be too terribly difficult to build offers. I don't mean that in like a... condescending way. Like for skilled marketers, it's not hard to analyze and look at. You know, what your customers are wanting, what's happening in the industry, what they are spending money on, what they aren't spending money on, and help reposition whatever it is that you offer. And I know in your case, you know, your horse training and things like that. To reposition your offers so that you now fit with what people want.
And that's essentially what needs to happen. Because there's a lot of reasons why that funnel might not be working. They first need to be able to diagnose it. Are they able to diagnose it? Have they come up with any ideas on that end? It doesn't feel like a clear diagnosis now because there's always a reason, but it's like always a changing reason. It's, it's never been a clear like this. It's something that could be wrong. Let's go fix this. Even when we have done that, then it's like, well, that didn't work. Well, it must be this other thing that's wrong. So it's just— it's just feeling like we can't get traction to really get to the problem. Like, we try, okay, we'll redo the webpage and see it. Is it a conversion thing?
We redo all the copy. Is it a copy thing? But it's, I feel like we're just chasing our tail with all these little things that's not fixing it. So it does feel like it's. It's, um... I don't know, like it's a deeper. A deeper disconnect that's happening with the customer or with the way that we're positioned in our market. Market. Hmm. Have you had a conversation with your marketing director on how confident are you that you can turn this around? It's not necessarily to fire them. It's like, maybe you need to bring in an outside consultant to look at it and have a fresh set of eyes and really take the time. to do the studies to figure out what's going on. Yeah. Yeah.
I haven't, I haven't had that conversation yet, but it's probably, we, we have consultants, but they're consultants like for the copy for the Facebook ads. Like it's, we don't have someone that comes in and looks at it systemically. Got it. I mean, even still, people ask me to come in to do coffee all the time. And I'm like, ooh, this isn't going to work. It's, it's. It all has to roll, roll up, right? We don't just like. So. Um, you know, I would maybe look at. talking to your marketing director and just saying, 'Hey, we have to come up with a plan.' This isn't something you're confident in. That's okay, but... Well, no, it's not okay. We need a plan. We need a plan.
And you're open to whatever, but whatever these little things here and there are isn't enough. And if you're not like. In a space where you confidently can have that conversation or know how to diagnose, then you need to bring somebody in that you trust that can just kind of give you an objective look at things and and it's not that they would know the answers, it's that they would know the process to find the answers. It's a very clear process to find the answers to figure it out. There's data points, there's ways that your customers communicate with you. There's feedback. There's research that you can do on your competition. There's voice of customer research. There's a whole litany of things that you can do. To figure that out.
All your marketing director needs to do is go and do those things to find it, but they might not know how. And even if they had that info, they might not know what to do with it. So you just have the wrong butt in seat or just don't have a butt in that seat that can do that. And so you might just need to bring in a consultant. That knows how to do that level of market research, look at your data and figure out, okay, here's your holes. And it might be small, it might be a huge, major and either way, I think just knowing is half the battle. Right. Yeah. Yeah. That makes a lot of sense. Thank you. And you know what?
In your industry, I feel like so many people are moving to the country. And you know, they've abandoned city life and, like, screw this. It's not worth it after the pandemic. So. One would think, like, with all these new found. Horse owners and landowners and stuff. You know, your business would be—doing a lot. Better or thriving because you guys have been in the space for a while. So that's just shocking to me. Um, not shocking, I'm just saying I feel like there's a real opportunity there. Potentially to tap into that persona. That's maybe new at horses. And maybe you guys are just targeting a more experienced persona when there's more opportunity over here. Potentially. I don't know. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah.
It's a lot of the people that I know that are in similar spaces are expanding too, which makes me think— like, it's not, it's not just market. It's definitely, it's more, there's more opportunity there in the market that it is a shrinking market and something that we're doing is not. Not catching it. Mm. Okay. Well, yeah, I mean, feel free, feel free to reach out. I think that, I think you need to get a, I think you need someone to come in and do a full. process, full audit of your growth engines and just. look out for what's going on and see. See what's going on in your market and help you reposition. or You know, it could be something small. I don't know. Yeah. Yeah, thank you. You're welcome. Awesome.
Thank you, Kali. Okay, cool. We're about 22 minutes left in the meeting. Jack is back. Jack says, 'Do you have any specific strategies you suggest for maximizing conversions from an email list in a commoditized industry? We have tested options like a newsletter, but coupon offerings are the only thing that have converted after years of different tests. Is this email list that you've built, or is this email list of people that you, like, an email list that you've purchased? It's an email list of people that have ordered with us previously. This is Jack from the trade-in company again. Yeah, no worries. Great questions. In your growth engine, I know you guys haven't built those yet. So yay.' Um, there's a part of the growth engine called 'advocate and promote,' right?
Are you automating? People sending you testimonials, are you advocating, are you automating getting referrals from people? Um, that. Purchasing or that— that might need to sell their phones or whatever, need extra cash. Yeah, we automate that portion. It's more about our actual email blast portion. So we have all our auto emails are doing well, like through the process of selling the device. And then the after-sale of, you know, getting reviews and those sorts of things. It's more of, you know, like over the next couple of years, I have, you know, we have a hundred thousand, 150,000 people on our. Email lists and we send an email and we convert, you know, like a percent of them or something along those lines. And so it's just converting higher percentage of the email list we have.
To sell again. Have you... What positioning are you taking on those emails? Are you like educating them or are you just trying to get them to re? like send, send your phone that you just got like a few months ago. And how's that? What is that? What are those segments looking like? So from an education perspective, we've not had any success with like educating on anything revolving around tech or anything else. Our customer base doesn't really care about tech. They just want to get money from us once a year when they upgrade. But we have educated them— like, 'Hey, there's a new phone coming out. In case you wanted to get it, that would be the only education piece we've used the most from us for us is just a straight-up coupon.
Then that's what's converted for us— the best in general. Like, if we don't have a coupon in the email, it's basically no one's going to like. Barely anyone will order with us. Hmm. Interesting. Huh? I mean... Yeah, I don't have anything on that. Ativa, if you do. Unless you're just asking for a referral or like. I can't think of anything else I would do in an email like that. If education isn't working, I think. I think education gets a bad rap because, you know, there's a lot of things that you can't track, right? And being top of mind is one of those things. And so while it doesn't look like your emails that are educational are converting, being top of mind actually does help because, let's say, they maybe weren't thinking about.
Upgrading or maybe they've been like a new phone came out or whatever and they're thinking about it, but just wasn't top of mind, like you know what I actually kind of want to go ahead and do it because you sent an email. They will go ahead and use your service even without a coupon. So I wouldn't necessarily discredit your educational emails and impacting their ability to convert, even if it doesn't seem like they are. Um, I think, you know, I said, I have this analogy about real estate, right? Like. You know, it's when you stop emailing those people when it's when they're ready to go buy a house and then they just happen to meet somebody at a bar that happens to be a real estate agent.
And it's like, oh man, I'm proud to remember that so-and-so, I talked to this person, I would have used them. And so. I think sometimes, just because of the life cycle of a phone, you know, just staying top of mind is super important. So I wouldn't discredit your educational emails as annoying as it may be to have to write to people that aren't paying any attention. Um... or so it seems, so that you don't constantly have to do coupons and stuff like that. Um. Okay, that's great feedback. I'm not necessarily sure what else I would do. Let me think on that. So I'll do some research and I'll try to have some answers for you next week. Thank you so much. Thanks again. Yeah. Awesome. Love it. Amara is such a rock star. Look at that. If you attend next Tuesday's call too, Jack, that would be awesome. Okay, sweet. Shazit is back. He says, 'How do you get your marketing team to work efficiently together?' Our marketing team has creative manager. My role is marketing creatives for content campaigns, content manager. The role is publishing content, managing social. influencer marketing. They have a campaign manager, media buying for Facebook ads. He says we want to maximize the team's ability to execute the marketing plan. Okay, so one of the things if they're all like independent contractors and in different parts of the world. One thing I often see is that they never see the full plan in their little piece of it and how. What they, I don't know if this is your case, just to the, I'm just saying one common thing I see is that when they don't see.
how it all works together, it can create the silo. Um. So unless they're like the person, so sometimes the conductor is like, okay, you do this, you do that, you do this, you do that, right? But had they known about all the things going on, they might have done their little piece of the world a little bit differently. And so I think it's super important. I, you know, as you go through Scalable, um There's a meeting rhythm. There's actually like an all-hands meeting that you're going to want to have with your team. That's not only just about your marketing, but your fulfillment too, your fulfillment engines and stuff like that as well. So those are incredibly, incredibly valuable and I did mine.
I don't know if it was a year ago, year and a half, two years, I don't know, a while ago when I first joined Scalable. It was so interesting how much my team did not know about the things that were going on, even though they're working on the same projects. I talk about all the things all the time. How much they did not know about how what they do affects other aspects of what we do. And so that's one way. Number two is your growth scorecard— super important. Um, making sure that everything in your growth engine, again, you haven't done these things yet. I'm championing them for a reason, though. Um, once you've built out your growth engine, everybody's clear on how growth and customers happen.
You can literally make sure that, you know, in the awareness stage, you have your KPIs identified. I cheat a little bit and sometimes it's just like, okay, we know that when we put out a certain amount of content, this is how it performs. So sometimes in the growth scorecard, I'll just put like making sure we have two blog posts a month. Because we know that if we can only get one done, that has a negative effect on things. Sometimes just making sure the things are actually getting done and executed will be on that scorecard as well. And and so on and so forth. And so really it's like when you start to move up a notch and you start to add more revenue to your goals, looking back at all the different channels, what levers are you going to pull?
To inch up towards that goal. And then you just test and make sure everybody knows. Like even if that's, you know, you're doing one additional thing and you're changing strategies, slightly. Everybody knows that there's been a slight change in strategy and it is to achieve growth. Um, having your systems documented is super important as well. Um, you know, getting everybody together, you know, to execute the marketing plan, just because you have a creative manager. Um, you have a, uh, a creative director or whatever, and a content manager. Having them in sync and on the same page is really important, even if they are doing different tasks, because there's probably some overlap there. Or even the creative manager might be able to support the content manager. In different ways that you might not have realized.
So I don't know if they all communicate in the same channels or not, or if you use Slack to maybe talk about different projects together and get input and stuff like that. What's your structure looking like? Yeah, well... First of all, we're not independent, meaning we do have an office, like we are working out of an office, except the media buyer or the campaign manager will be working remotely. We mainly communicate through WhatsApp. And also recently we started using Trello. To kind of set deadlines and things like that. I just— You know, I do, I'm involved in the marketing planning process completely. And what we normally do, our process is kind of like this. So before the month starts, we would get together and set the marketing.
I would get them to do a pre-planning. Based on certain questions, and then based on that, we'll do a marketing plan for the entire next month or the next quarter. And we would set what type of contents. We need to publish or we need to create. We set that in the marketing plan. And then also we set— specifically which campaigns we're going to be running on Facebook, because most of our sales is generated through Facebook ads. Um, or just through our branding, because now we have created a brand in our country. So we get some organic, I guess, branding-type of sales from that. Um, but yeah, we're pretty bullish on content. You know, we try to. We try to publish every single day. We have sort of a content rhythm.
Once a week, we would uh, we would share like an informational video, which is maybe one or two minutes long, which is obviously recorded way ahead. We do blog posts once a week. So these are KPIs that we already have set and we're doing pretty well. But now I'm putting together the team, like I said, that I want to step back a little bit. I don't want to be so involved. When I'm really involved, all of these things are going great. But I just want to make sure that they are able to execute. You know, the marketing plan by themselves, you know, meeting the deadlines, communicating with each other— those are some of the challenges that I can kind of foresee. That would happen if I step back. Got it.
How do you kind of... you know, get them to work more efficiently and effectively. On these on executing the plan. And also coming up with better ideas. Sorry. And also coming up with more innovative ideas. You know what I mean? Like. I have already put together a I guess, a structure that works. And yeah, I'm sure they can follow that. But I would also want them to come up with innovative ideas or more content ideas. Um, And. And. discuss among each other and execute Do it and test. And sort of go from there. Yeah. So I have a couple different, um, tools that I use in my agency. One of the things, I don't know if you have a project manager or if you're just a superhuman project manager marketing
person and you've been kind of project managing everybody you might have, I guess, I, I, I would be the project manager yeah, so maybe in the future I would have to look for a project manager, but as of right now I am the project manager. Yeah, you're superhuman. So you might have a few roles baked into you that you might need to fill before you step away. So get a project manager in place that can make sure all the tasks are inside of your Trello since you use Trello. That person should use Trello and can keep the traffic moving. Um, the other thing we do is, um, for ideas, we do what we call is a hot sheet.
So one of our like, assist marketing assistants or interns, or we don't have any interns actually, but whenever some of our entry-level marketers will go in and we'll look at like comments on Facebook, we'll look at Twitter conversations and listen. We'll look at Cora. We'll look at all these different things for the week. For a week period of time and then just create like a huge sheet of ideas. and um like feedback from a webinar or feed like questions from a webinar or whatever or um just whatever we find out there that people are talking about to help inform some of the content that we're writing. So as long as you have a system for creating ideas, and have a place like a repo of where those ideas should live and where people should enter those ideas, and have someone really challenged to bring those ideas to the table.
That's one way you can kind of get them off of you. Cause, if you've just been kind of doing it, cause, like, you're in the business every single day now during your next all hands meeting, it's like, okay, well here we're going to do this exercise on a weekly basis and start. It'll start to backfill. And you'll have more content ideas and you'll know what to do with. Because some of those ideas will turn into 10 ideas. and at that point, it's just prioritizing and so that's one of the things that we do for one of our for some of our bigger clients—I just have a boatload of content. that we write. and things like that. If one of your people can be promoted, and given more responsibility like your creative manager.
Um. Give them some reins and say, okay, so what are your sum of ideas? You put together the marketing plan. You've seen me do it. Run this meeting and see how it goes. And then you just shadow them and just be their support. And see what happens, see where they fall short. Are they, you know, do they need, do you need an extra person that they've been analysts? That can come in and handle your reporting, even if it's not like a full-time role, but just someone who can just come in and like. Make sure things are on track as far as your KPIs and stuff that you guys have together. Um, Those are some ideas. So, project manager, maybe having an analyst and then. Doing some sort of having some sort of process for ideation.
That's based on something. Anything, whether it's new products or customer feedback, feedback out in the wild based on competitors. Stuff and things like that that people are doing. Even like seasons and stuff like holidays and stuff like that. That's the way we do our content planning. I call it like the rocks, pebbles, sand, water concept where it's like the things that are removable, like your holidays and even industry-specific holidays. You plan those first. If you plan that three months out, at least. You can come up with a really fantastic campaign. And then this, you know, pebbles are like the things that you do consistently. Um, maybe you do a webinar once a month, or you said you do an info thing once a week, throw those in there.
Cause those are consistent and they happen all the time. Your sand is kind of like. The little ideas that you guys come up with every month. And those are the things that you would use a hot sheet for or whatever. And then the water is the things that come up out of nowhere. That's like 10% of your stuff. You don't want to be having to think of stuff during the week or whenever when you're trying to run content. But really, just give them a framework for planning. Um and just have them take it away. You can steal mine. I just kind of gave you one. This is recorded, so you can go back and listen to it. But some of the stuff that you do so easily— just kind of maybe create some frameworks around it that they can follow.
That should help. Got it. Thank you so much. Just a follow-up question on that. Hmm. So, you know, often, like, let's say when I'm browsing social media or something, like I would see maybe a brand that's... because we're based in Asia, but maybe I'll see a brand in USA or Canada or something doing certain offers. Or like, maybe they just have a really good creative, or maybe I just saw a TikTok video, which I really liked, that maybe we could. We could shoot something like that. So I would probably send them to send. Send it to them on WhatsApp. Like, 'Oh, hey, this is a good inspirational content that we could potentially do.' But then what happens a lot of the times is it just gets lost. There.
Like they'll be like, 'Yeah, it's a good idea,' you know, and then maybe that's not documented or. That just. That never comes to the reality. So how do you sort of. come up with, I guess, a workflow or a process to get ideas. into reality. Make it someone's job. If no one owns new things, then no one's going to own it. They're going to be like, 'Okay, great.' You tell us when you want to do it. Cause right now you own it. So have someone else own it. So if you have a creative manager, um, like okay, you now own this. You now are supposed to take. I give you guys a ton of ideas, so you know, if you don't have any, use my ideas, but I expect you to bring new ideas to the table and fill in this marketing plan that we do every, every month or every quarter or whatever it is.
Um, and And put them on for taking some of those things that you bring up and coming up with a plan to execute. Now, some of those things that might not make sense to execute just because it's cool, but it might be something that you think about. Or, you know. Just you know, at least keep on the horizon. Um and things like that, but like we actually have a sheet that we keep that has comments that we find on other ads that we see related to that client in the industry, like 'Oh okay'— here's what customers are saying, 'Here's what customers are saying they want,' 'Here's what customers are complaining about.' Summon someone out to start gathering that data. And then bring it to your meetings and make sure that they actually use it for something.
Um. And it just kind of depends on how your team is positioned. If they're just order takers and they're not used to really. Like stepping up and, um, in that way, you're gonna have to really yeah, they're not—I'm empowering they're not. Yeah, or hire someone that's already empowered to do that. It's really hard to take someone who's used to being an order taker and implementer and moving them into a leadership position because there's two things that happen. Just because you are a power doer doesn't mean you're a power leader, right? Like I've seen so many people who are amazing at execution, completely just fail at managing people because it's like: You have to manage deadlines, priorities, emotions, all the things.
So you might need to just bring in someone that's already empowered and knows how to take the treasure trove of awesome that you've already created and make it even more awesome. Got it. Thank you so much. You're welcome. Sweet. Thank you so much, Amara. Uh, looks like we have one more question— last question of the day. From Alejandro, he says, what are some of the common mistakes you see e-commerce companies make when they're focusing on growth, i. e., premature spending, cash flow, management, etc.? All of the above. I remember one of my first e-comm companies that I worked on when I started my agency full-time. I mean, they're, they're paying me like $500 a month and he was selling his product out of the trunk of his car.
And then we got our first retailer. And then we got Zoomies and Tillys, which, if you're not from the U. S., it's a big box like retail store for like teenage stuff. I don't know, like teenagers go there all the time. And they were ordering hundreds of thousands of units. A quarter so their volume increased from like few thousand a week to like hundreds of thousands a week. It was crazy. So not proper planning, like not logistically planning, making sure their factories can produce enough goods quickly enough. Especially if they have a growth strategy, like if you're trying to get into big box retail, like getting enough financing so you can get enough product. Because the first thing... The biggest hesitation of retailers. I don't know. Oh, you're talking about e-comm.
Just kidding. Um, Scratch that. But even still, like not planning for volume, like. Um, holidays and stuff like Black Friday. Yes, still valid actually yeah. So like Black Friday, you know, all the big consumer holidays, not doing proper planning like six months out, especially if you're getting a product overseas and stuff. Um bulk planning so that you get enough products that you can lower your product costs and things like that and get more margin, because remember your ad costs go up so if we're using advertising to drive revenue, your ad costs go up during that period. So get your product in early and get it get it cheap as cheaply as possible— not cheap, but as inexpensively as possible, so that you can make up that difference when everybody's hunting and killing for the same customers at a higher price, because obviously that revenue is going up.
Um, not increasing pricing like they should. I think that's important. We all know inflation is like running away. Know companies, small businesses, especially for some reason, feel bad all the bigger companies are doing it. If you got to raise your prices to survive, then do it, right? Premature spending is definitely an epidemic. Especially spending on marketing. It's freaking weird. I don't know why, but I see it a lot with e-commerce companies. Not having financing in place when they need it. Thank you. And, you know, I know like Shopify and a lot of other like. Stripe have like merchant financing where they base it off of your volume. But just having better financing terms, like lines of credit and stuff with their bank, and not having that having that set up so that when you have those periods and like for holidays and stuff, you can purchase.
At a low enough interest rate to where it's not going to affect your bottom line. So yeah, there's, there's definitely a lot like. Not planning marketing ahead of time for holidays and things like that and industry-specific stuff. I could go on forever. Um, But yeah, like logistics, not, you know, packaging, having, you know, more employees to help like selling a hundred pieces a month and selling 10,000 pieces a month changes everything about your operation. So I'm not planning for that. and having the right logistics partners and things like that. Big issues. Are there any different ROAS or admetrics you typically look for for physical goods? I think that's... Bye, Jack. Great to meet you. I know we have to jump in a minute. I don't know.
I mean, I do really good ROAS. I do between, if I'm not doing, so it's all depends on your profit margin, right? So like, if you have like 10% margin in your profits, in your company, in your stuff, then you need like 10 to 1 ROAS, like period, or else you're breaking even, right? It just depends on your profit margin, really. Yeah, I would say I couldn't answer that unless I knew what your profit margin is. Um. On your stuff, but I I try to reach for anything above five five between five and ten. Um. Yeah. And there's a lot of things to consider, like return on ad spend might not be the best metric, especially with the seven-day window on Facebook. Look at your lifetime value, cost per acquisition, your lifetime value.
That might be more important. I wouldn't say more important, but that's an additional thing to look into. Over and above ROAS if your products are expensive and there's a longer decision cycle. Oh, okay. Got it. Got it. Got it. Okay. I know we're running over on time. I don't want to get yelled at. No, no, you're fine. If you're good to keep helping. You're welcome, Alejandro.
